metadata.summary: Five-step process for strategic roadmaps: format selection, themes linked to objectives, confidence levels, audience rendering. Core rule: every initiative links to objective ID.

content.title: Roadmap Design
content.summary: A five-step process for building a product roadmap as a strategic communication tool, not a delivery schedule. Covers four roadmap formats (timeline, Now-Next-Later, outcome-based, kanban) with a selection matrix, theme-based organisation, confidence levels, and audience-specific rendering (internal vs external). The core rule: every initiative links to one objective ID; orphaned initiatives are strategy leakage.

why.p: Roadmaps that list features with dates always slip and erode trust. The format mismatch is the root cause: a timeline roadmap implies commitment; a Now-Next-Later roadmap signals intent. Choosing the wrong format for the audience turns every missed date into a broken promise. The theme-plus-objective structure prevents the roadmap from degenerating into a backlog; confidence levels prevent "Later" from becoming a graveyard.

list.item: Communicating a 1-4 quarter plan to internal team, investors, or customers.
Multiple stakeholders need a single source of truth linking strategy → themes → initiatives.
Choosing between roadmap formats — use the selection matrix.
Onboarding a new contractor or PM who needs a one-page direction document.
Less than 4 weeks of work — roadmap overhead exceeds value; use a sprint plan or release notes.
Pure exploration phase before PMF — explicit roadmaps create anchors that bias discovery.
Highly volatile environments (hackathon, week-by-week pivots) — keep a backlog.
Feature commitment to a single customer — that is a contract, not a roadmap.

section.name: four-roadmap-types
section.p: Four roadmap types exist, each suited to different contexts. The selection matrix based on uncertainty level and external-sharing requirements determines which to use.

list.item: Timeline roadmap: date-based, quarters or months, committed work. Use when: external sharing required with committed deliverables, low uncertainty, high team maturity.
Now-Next-Later roadmap: priority sequence, no dates. Use when: internal or limited external sharing, medium uncertainty, any team maturity.
Outcome-based roadmap: metric targets per theme. Use when: strategic alignment focus, high uncertainty, external sharing acceptable at theme level.
Kanban roadmap: live work state. Use when: internal teams only, pure execution visibility, no strategic communication needed.

rule.statement: Choose the format by uncertainty level first, then external-sharing requirement. High uncertainty + external sharing = outcome-based or Now-Next-Later (themes only). Low uncertainty + external sharing = timeline.
rule.rationale: Format mismatch is the root cause of roadmap trust failures. A timeline roadmap in a high-uncertainty context creates commitments that cannot be kept, destroying stakeholder trust on every slip.

example.title: Format selection matrix
example.code: Uncertainty high: outcome-based or Now-Next-Later Uncertainty medium: Now-Next-Later Uncertainty low: timeline or Kanban External sharing required: timeline (committed) or outcome/Now-Next-Later (theme level only) Internal only: any format
example.note: The matrix overrides personal preference or stakeholder bias. Let uncertainty and sharing requirement drive the decision.

rule.statement: For a solo founder, the roadmap must fit on one screen. If it does not, it is a backlog with a roadmap header, not a roadmap.
rule.rationale: A roadmap communicates strategic direction. Anything requiring scrolling has too much detail for strategic communication and too little for task execution.

section.name: five-step-design-process
section.p: The roadmap design process consists of five sequential steps: strategy input, format selection, theme definition, horizon population with confidence, and stakeholder context. Each step depends on the previous one.

rule.statement: Step 1 — Gather four inputs before touching the roadmap format: company/product vision, business goals for the period, user research insights, and technical constraints. Output: clear objectives for the roadmap period.
rule.rationale: A roadmap without strategy inputs is a backlog sorted by recency. The objectives from this step are the connective tissue between every initiative and the business direction.

rule.statement: Step 2 — Use the selection matrix as a deterministic first step. Do not let the audience or personal preference drive the format choice — let uncertainty level and external-sharing requirement drive it.
rule.rationale: Format chosen by audience preference produces a timeline roadmap for high-uncertainty work because stakeholders always want dates. The matrix overrides this bias.

rule.statement: Step 3 — Group all planned work into 3–5 themes. For each theme write: name, which objective it drives, why it is prioritised now, and 2–3 example initiatives. No theme should be orphaned from an objective.
rule.rationale: Themes without objectives are backlog categories, not strategic groupings. The objective link is what prevents "Later" from becoming a permanent graveyard of low-priority features.

rule.statement: Step 4 — Assign each initiative a confidence level: High (committed and scoped), Medium (planned, needs scoping), Low (exploring, tentative). At least 30% of items must be Medium or Low — if everything is High, the labelling is decoration.
rule.rationale: Uniform High confidence strips the signal from Now-Next-Later. Stakeholders learn to ignore confidence levels that are never Low, and the roadmap loses its communication function.

rule.statement: Limit Now to 5 initiatives maximum. Past 5, the team is effectively planning a backlog sprint, not a strategic now-horizon.
rule.rationale: Teams can execute 3–5 significant initiatives in parallel before coordination costs outweigh benefits. More than 5 in Now signals that priorities have not been made.

rule.statement: Step 5 — For each horizon, document: why you chose these initiatives, what you are NOT doing and why, dependencies and risks, and how success will be measured. The "not doing" section is mandatory — it stops scope creep better than any priority list.
rule.rationale: Without explicit exclusions, every reasonable idea becomes a candidate for this quarter. The not-doing list also creates a retrospective record of scope decisions, which is invaluable for explaining past trade-offs.

section.name: maintenance-and-publishing
section.p: A roadmap is a living document. Maintenance rules and audience-specific publishing rules keep it coherent and trustworthy over time.

rule.statement: Every initiative must link to one objective ID. Initiatives without an objective link are strategy leakage — remove them or create the missing objective.
rule.rationale: Orphaned initiatives represent work that is in the plan without strategic justification. They accumulate over time and eventually dominate the roadmap without anyone being able to explain why.

rule.statement: Update cadence: monthly review of the initiative list, quarterly rewrite of the full document. Never rewrite the whole doc each sprint — that destroys the historical diff signal.
rule.rationale: Quarter-to-quarter diffs are the primary retrospective artefact. Frequent complete rewrites make it impossible to see how the plan evolved or why certain items moved.

rule.statement: Always include an "Explicitly Not Doing" section with reasons. It doubles as a future review log for stakeholder questions like "why didn't we build X?"
rule.rationale: Stakeholders re-request excluded items constantly. A documented reason closes each request with a reference instead of reopening the debate.

rule.statement: Maintain two artefacts: internal roadmap (full detail: outcomes, owners, confidence, metrics) and external roadmap (themes and benefits only, no confidence levels, no owners, no metric targets). Sync via script, not copy-paste.
rule.rationale: Sharing confidence levels externally signals uncertainty in a way that customers interpret as incompetence. Sharing metric targets creates contractual expectations. Sharing owners exposes internal structure.

rule.statement: Never let an agent ship the external roadmap without human review. Review for commitments, NDAs, and competitive signalling — LLMs cannot assess business risk context.
rule.rationale: External roadmaps are marketing documents with legal and competitive implications. An LLM cannot assess NDA scope, competitive sensitivity, or contractual interpretation.

section.name: examples-and-antipatterns
section.p: Real examples from B2B SaaS and solo products show how roadmaps balance depth and simplicity. Antipatterns highlight common failures.

example.title: B2B SaaS roadmap
example.code:
Now (Q1): onboarding improvements (reduce time to value), API v2 launch (enterprise requirement), performance optimization (load times). Each tied to objective: "Improve activation rate to 40%".
Next (Q2): team collaboration features (confidence: High), Slack integration (confidence: Medium), advanced permissions (confidence: Medium). Depends on: API v2 stable.
Later: mobile app (pending user research validation), AI-powered insights (exploring, no commitment).
Explicitly Not Doing: custom themes, white-labelling — not on enterprise ICP roadmap this year.
example.note: Each initiative has confidence and objective. Next has mixed confidence (not all High). Explicit not-doing section present.

example.title: Solo product roadmap
example.code:
Now: core feature polish toward MVP, payment integration, basic analytics.
Next: email automation, template library, referral program.
Later: mobile app, team features, marketplace.
Not doing: multi-language support — insufficient international user demand to justify.
example.note: Fits one screen. Minimal theme structure. Not-doing has a reason.

antipattern.description: Feature list with specific dates for every item.
antipattern.reason: Dates imply commitment. When any date slips, stakeholder trust erodes. Use confidence levels instead of dates for everything beyond "Now".

antipattern.description: No strategic connection — initiatives not linked to objectives.
antipattern.reason: Orphaned initiatives accumulate and eventually dominate the roadmap without anyone being able to justify why. Every initiative must have an objective ID.
If items never graduate from Later to Next, stakeholders stop believing the roadmap is real. Review Later quarterly and either promote, archive, or move to not-doing with a reason.

antipattern.description: Same roadmap shared with engineering, exec, and customers.
antipattern.reason: Each audience needs a different abstraction level. Engineering needs initiative detail; exec needs theme-level strategy; customers need benefit language with no internal jargon.

section.name: agentic-constraints
section.p: When using AI agents to generate or refine roadmaps, explicit constraints override default LLM behavior.

rule.statement: When prompting an LLM for roadmap generation: force confidence distribution (now=mostly High, next=mostly Medium, later=mostly Low), cap themes at 3–5, cap Now initiatives at 5, mark not_doing as a required field.
rule.rationale: LLMs default to "everything is High confidence", produce 8+ themes, and silently drop the not-doing column because it produces no generative content. Explicit constraints in the prompt override these defaults.

rule.statement: When the LLM is fed a previous quarter's roadmap as context, explicitly instruct it to identify what changed since the last review — otherwise it will simply restate the prior roadmap.
rule.rationale: Models are trained to be helpful, which defaults to continuity. Without explicit change-detection instructions, the agent produces a cosmetically different version of the same document.

template.purpose: Now-Next-Later roadmap with vision, themed initiative slots, confidence levels, dependencies, success metrics.
Quarterly outcome roadmap with objectives, initiatives by theme, explicit not-doing, risks, review schedule.
Customer-facing roadmap: benefits language, no confidence levels or owners, subject-to-change disclaimer.
Python validator: checks required keys, Now initiative count, not_doing presence, confidence and objective_id per initiative.
